cover.title: Agentic AI
cover.key: Welcome everyone. Today I want to talk about something that's reshaping how companies work — and how it's going to reshape how we work at Temper.
points: Not a technical talk — applies to every role
By the end: what agentic AI is, why it matters, what you can do this week
cover.transition: Let's start with something we all already know...

ai-everywhere.title: AI Is Already Everywhere
ai-everywhere.key: We've been using AI for years without thinking about it — Google search, spam filters, Netflix recommendations. But what's happening right now is a different kind of shift.
points: Three stages on screen — we've lived in the first two for years
The new part: AI that doesn't just suggest, but goes and does things for you
This talk is about that third column
ai-everywhere.transition: So what does that evolution actually look like?

spectrum.title: The AI Spectrum
spectrum.key: Think of it as three levels. Most of you have used the first two already.
points: Chatbot: you ask, it answers, you close the tab (early ChatGPT)
Copilot: it helps while you drive (Gemini in Docs, GitHub Copilot)
Agent: you say "handle this" — it plans, decides, and acts
The jump from copilot to agent is what everyone is racing toward
spectrum.transition: Let's make that concrete. What actually makes AI 'agentic'?

what-is-agentic.title: So What Makes AI "Agentic"?
what-is-agentic.key: Here's a way to think about it: these four capabilities are things we already do as humans every day. Agentic AI just does the same thing.
points: Perceive: check email, read the ticket, open the doc
Reason: what's the best approach, what steps do I take?
Act: write the email, update the sheet, move the card
Learn: next time, do it faster because you remember what worked
An agent does this loop in seconds instead of hours
what-is-agentic.transition: Let me show you a side-by-side to make this really clear.

chatbot-vs-agent.title: Chatbot vs. Agent
chatbot-vs-agent.key: If you take one thing from this talk, let it be this slide. This is the core difference.
points: Left: chatbot is isolated — red X marks, can't reach anything, stuck in a box
Right: agent is connected to your actual tools — reads and writes
Librarian vs. chef analogy: one tells you the recipe, the other cooks the meal
chatbot-vs-agent.transition: Now think about your own day. Where does this matter most?

integration-layer.title: You Are the Integration Layer
integration-layer.key: Here's something I think everyone in this room will recognise. Look at these tools — you probably use most of them every day.
points: The tools are great — the problem is they don't talk to each other
You are the human copy-paste layer between them
Every arrow on screen = your time spent on glue work
This is exactly what agents eliminate
integration-layer.transition: So what happens when we actually remove those arrows? Let's look at the impact.

impact-divider.title: The Impact
impact-divider.key: So that's what agentic AI is. Now let's talk about why it matters — what the actual impact looks like.
impact-divider.cue: Brief pause. Section divider — let the image breathe.

before-after.title: Before & After Agents
before-after.key: I picked three examples that I think everyone here deals with, regardless of your team.
points: Status reports: 45 min gathering data → 2 min reviewing an AI draft
Meeting follow-ups: manual notes & actions → auto-created, you approve
Ticket triage: hours of sorting → instant categorisation & routing
These are workflows we can build today — not science fiction
Honest note: the first time takes longer — you're setting up context and teaching the agent. But once it's dialled in, the time savings compound every single time you run it
before-after.transition: And we're not the only ones doing this...

already-happening.title: It's Already Happening
already-happening.key: I want to show you what real companies are doing with this. These are not pilot programs — these are production results.
points: Klarna: resolution 11 min → 2 min, 2x revenue, half the workforce
Google: 50% of code written by AI, +10% engineering velocity
Microsoft: $13B AI revenue, 85% of employees use Copilot daily
Reddit: 46% of support automated, response 9 min → 1.4 min
ING (NL): 90% of AI projects to production, agentic mortgages in 2026
Booking.com (NL): $700M saved, +73% partner satisfaction
already-happening.transition: And the market numbers are just as striking...

tipping-point.title: The Tipping Point
tipping-point.key: If those company examples weren't convincing enough, look at the market data. We're past the tipping point.
points: 79% of companies have adopted AI agents — not experimenting, adopted (PwC)
Market: $5B → $199B by 2034, 43% annual growth
40% of enterprise apps will have AI agents by end of 2026, up from <5% (Gartner)
This isn't coming — it's already here
tipping-point.transition: OK, so it's big. But how does it actually work? I'll break it down into three pillars.

how-divider.title: How It Works
how-divider.key: Now that we know what it is and why it matters — let's get into the how. I'm going to break this into three pillars.

connections-divider.title: Connections
connections-divider.key: The first pillar is Connections. Without connections, AI is just a fancy chatbot.
points: If AI can't see your data, it only works with what you paste in — chatbot with extra steps

why-connections.title: Why Connections Matter
why-connections.key: This slide shows the fundamental difference. On the left is what most people do today. On the right is where we're going.
points: Without: every interaction is a one-off, nothing persists, nothing improves
With: AI reads your tools directly, output grounded in real data, workflows persist
These connections already exist — the question is whether we use them
why-connections.transition: So how do these connections actually work? There's a new standard called MCP.

what-is-mcp.title: What Is MCP?
what-is-mcp.key: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. The easiest way to think about it is: USB-C, but for AI.
points: Before USB-C: every phone had a different charger. MCP = one protocol, every tool
Open standard — not locked to any vendor
100+ tools supported and growing weekly
Not just reading — agents can create, update, send, move. Full read-write
what-is-mcp.transition: Let me show you what's already connected...

mcp-in-action.title: MCP In Action
mcp-in-action.key: These are tools you already use — and they already have MCP connectors. AI can read and write to all of them right now.
points: AI in the center, each tool plugs in through MCP
We can also build our own MCP server for any internal API
mcp-in-action.cue: Let the animation play. Tools appear one by one on the orbit.
mcp-in-action.transition: But connections alone aren't enough. You need control. That brings us to pillar two.

human-control-divider.title: Human in the Loop
human-control-divider.key: Pillar two is about control. Because giving AI access to your tools is powerful — but it also needs guardrails.

human-in-loop.title: Human in the Loop
human-in-loop.key: I want to be very clear about something: agentic does not mean autonomous. The best AI systems keep humans at the decision points that matter.
points: Flow: AI collects → AI drafts → human reviews → action taken
Sweet spot is in the middle — not fully manual, not fully autonomous
Start supervised, automate gradually as trust builds
human-in-loop.transition: But not everything needs the same level of oversight...

trust-gradient.title: The Trust Gradient
trust-gradient.key: This is one of the most practical slides in the whole talk. Not every task needs the same level of human involvement.
points: Internal data summaries → fully autonomous, low risk
Slack updates → mostly autonomous, quick glance
Client-facing emails → human reviews, reputation at stake
Financial decisions, code deployments → human approves, stakes too high
Think about risk level first, then match automation to it
Important: even 'fully autonomous' tasks need validation early on — AI can confidently get things wrong. Spot-check outputs until you trust the pattern, then let it run
trust-gradient.transition: Now pillar three — this is the one that makes everything else work.

context-divider.title: Context Engineering
context-divider.key: The third pillar is Context. And this is honestly the most important one — because connections and control mean nothing if the AI doesn't understand what you need.

what-is-context.title: What Is Context?
what-is-context.key: Context is everything the AI knows when it starts working for you. And here's the thing — you're already managing context, you just don't call it that.
points: Pasting background info or adding "professional tone" = context management
Goal: set it up once so AI always has the right information
Context has a limit — the skill is giving the right context, not all of it
what-is-context.transition: You're actually already doing a version of this...

context-before-after.title: Context Engineering: Before & After
context-before-after.key: I bet everyone here has rewritten a ChatGPT prompt to get a better answer. That's prompt engineering. You're already doing it.
points: Left (now): generic prompt → paste notes → OK result → close tab → start from scratch next time
Right (where we're going): rich instruction, AI already knows your team/tools/conventions, you review and approve
"Engineering" just means setting things up so AI works better without repeating yourself
The setup takes effort upfront — writing down your conventions, defining your formats — but it compounds. Every future interaction benefits from context you set up once
context-before-after.transition: Let me show you the layers of what makes up good context.

four-layers.title: The 4 Layers of Context
four-layers.key: There are four layers that make up the context an agent works with. Think of it as a stack — each layer adds richness.
points: Instructions: role, tone, format, rules, guardrails
Knowledge: team processes, conventions, domain expertise
Memory: past decisions, preferences, history
Tools: live connections to Notion, Sheets, calendars, APIs
More layers = better output. The gap between generic and eerily accurate is usually just context
four-layers.transition: Now, let's address the elephant in the room.

changes-your-work.title: This Changes How You Work. Not Whether You Work.
changes-your-work.key: I know what some of you might be thinking. "Is this going to replace my job?" Let me be direct: no.
points: AI amplifies what you can do — doesn't replace the need for you
Agents handle busywork: data gathering, formatting, copy-pasting
You focus on judgment: strategy, creativity, relationships, decisions
Same quality, less time. Days → hours. Weeks → days
More with the same team — not the same with fewer people
changes-your-work.cue: Quote appears as hero first. Press to reveal the full slide.
changes-your-work.transition: And here's the exciting part — you don't need to be a developer to build with AI.

everyone-can-build.title: You Already Have the Hardest Skill
everyone-can-build.key: Here's something I want everyone to hear: the hardest part of AI isn't the technology. It's knowing what problem to solve. And that's something you already have.
points: You know which report takes 3 hours, where handoffs break, where the bottleneck is
Describing a solution to AI = explaining it to a new colleague
Technical barrier is shrinking — domain expertise remains irreplaceable
everyone-can-build.transition: Now let's bring this home. How does this fit at Temper?

temper-divider.title: At Temper
temper-divider.key: OK, so that's the big picture. Now let's talk about us — where does this fit at Temper?

our-opportunity.title: Where We Can Start
our-opportunity.key: I picked three areas where I think we can see immediate impact. These are things every team deals with.
points: Reporting: agent drafts from your tools, you review in minutes
Support triage: categorise, draft response, route — team approves
Meeting follow-ups: transcript → action items → tasks, automatically
Start small with one workflow. The first run is slower — you're teaching the agent. But every run after gets faster and more accurate
our-opportunity.transition: And there are even more possibilities across the company...

more-use-cases.title: More Use Cases
more-use-cases.key: These are just four more examples, but honestly the list is endless. Think about what your version of this might be.
points: Data analysis: agent pulls data, surfaces patterns, gives you the story
Documentation: agent watches for decisions, keeps docs in sync
Onboarding: AI buddy that knows our processes, answers questions instantly
Project kickoffs: reviews past projects, gathers context, drafts a plan
more-use-cases.transition: Let's talk about the tools we already have access to.

agentic-tools.title: Agentic Tools at Temper
agentic-tools.key: These are the agentic tools we've approved for use at Temper. You can see the access level for each one.
points: Gemini: everyone, connects to entire Google Workspace
Developers: Gemini CLI, Claude Code, Cursor, Firebender
All approved and in the AI tools catalogue — no permission needed to start
agentic-tools.transition: But you don't need any of these to start. Gemini is already set up.

gemini.title: Gemini: Start Right Now
gemini.key: If you take out your phone right now and go to gemini.google.com with your Temper account, you'll see it can already read all your Google Workspace data.
points: No setup, no API keys — already connected to Docs, Calendar, Gmail, Drive, Keep, Tasks, Meet
Try: "What meetings do I have tomorrow?" or "Summarise the last 5 emails from this client"
Lowest-effort, highest-impact thing you can try today
gemini.transition: And for automations — there's something even more powerful.

workspace-studio.title: Google Workspace Studio
workspace-studio.key: This is brand new. You describe what you want to automate in plain English, and Gemini builds an agent that runs across your Workspace.
points: Triggers from: emails, meeting transcripts, sheet changes, Drive, calendar, forms
No code — describe the workflow, it builds it
Available on all Temper accounts now — scan QR to try
workspace-studio.cue: Point to QR code on screen.
workspace-studio.transition: And beyond agents — there's AI already built into the tools you use every day.

ai-built-in.title: AI Built Into Your Tools
ai-built-in.key: Before we even get to agents — there's AI already running inside tools you use every day. You might not even realise it.
points: Slack AI: summarise a channel, catch up on 200 messages in 30 seconds
Asana AI: auto status updates and subtasks
NotebookLM: upload any doc, have a conversation with it
These aren't agents — no autonomous actions. But great starting point to get comfortable
ai-built-in.transition: Now, with all this power comes responsibility. Let's talk about the risks.

hidden-dangers.title: The Hidden Dangers
hidden-dangers.key: I want to be honest about the risks. The same capability that makes agents useful also makes them dangerous if used carelessly.
points: Data absorption: confidential doc uploaded to third-party AI → could end up in training data
PII cascading: agent chains tasks, a worker ID from one step leaks into a Slack message or log
Also: agents can get things wrong confidently — a miscategorised ticket or a hallucinated metric can go unnoticed if nobody checks
Mitigations: minimum access, human approval for high-stakes, audit trails
hidden-dangers.transition: This brings us to a core Temper value...

safety-quote.title: Data Safety Quote
safety-quote.key: This is something I feel strongly about. At Temper, our whole business is built on trust — trust from workers, trust from businesses. We can't afford to be careless with AI.
points: Agents need the same vetting as any team member — background check, clear boundaries
Freedom without guardrails isn't freedom — it's chaos
safety-quote.cue: Let the reveals land one by one. This is the emotional core of the safety message.
safety-quote.transition: So what does that look like in practice?

safety-checklist.title: AI Data Safety Checklist
safety-checklist.key: We've put together a simple checklist. Before any agentic project goes live, run through these five questions.
points: Guiding question: "Does the agent actually need to see this data?"
Key items: redact PII, least privilege access, HITL for finances/deletion, training opt-out enabled, audit trails on every tool call
Don't read every item — they can see the list on screen
safety-checklist.transition: And when you find new tools — here's the process.

discovering-tools.title: Discovering New Tools
discovering-tools.key: We're in a discovery phase. New AI tools pop up every week. Here's how we handle that together.
points: Try it personally first — no company data
Document: what is it, what problem, how you'd use it at Temper
Ping Radu, William, or Annabel before connecting company data — not a gate, a conversation
Our policies grow as we discover — every tool you explore helps everyone
discovering-tools.cue: Point to QR code — Temper AI Policy on Notion.
discovering-tools.transition: Now... I have a confession to make.

plot-twist.title: Plot Twist
plot-twist.key: Everything you've seen in this presentation — every slide, every animation, every line of code — was built by an AI agent.
points: No PowerPoint, no hand-coded CSS — built through conversation with Claude Code
Terminal on screen shows actual commands used
plot-twist.cue: Let "Wait For It..." land big. Pause. Then press to reveal. Let the typewriter animation run.
plot-twist.transition: And here's what's interesting — it used exactly the same three pillars we just talked about.

how-made.title: How This Was Made
how-made.key: This presentation is the proof of everything I just told you. It was built with the exact same three pillars.
points: Connections: reads/writes files, controls browser, runs commands — all via MCP
Human Control: every change guided by feedback — I directed, AI executed
Rich Context: brand guidelines, color palette, icon library, design specs → looks like Temper, not generic AI

full-picture.title: Bringing It All Together
full-picture.key: Here's the formula. Three ingredients, one outcome: automation you can actually trust.
points: Right tools + human control + rich context
Goes from copy-pasting between tools to reviewing outputs that are 90% done
full-picture.transition: And here's what you can do starting this week.

start-this-week.title: Start This Week
start-this-week.key: I don't want this to be a talk you forget by tomorrow. Here are four things you can do this week.
points: 1. Try an agent — build a Gemini Gem for something you do weekly
2. Write down your context — conventions, processes, formats → fuel for any AI tool
3. Design a human gate — pick one workflow, decide where a person reviews
4. Share what you build — post in #ai-workflows on Slack, one person's workflow saves the team hours
start-this-week.transition: And with that — I'd love to hear your questions.

qa.title: Questions?
qa.key: Thank you all. I'm genuinely excited about what we can build with this. Let's figure it out together.
points: Open the floor
If silence: "Pull out your phone, gemini.google.com, Temper account. Start right now."
